Journal.Title: Journal of bodywork and movement therapies

Article.ArticleTitle: Effect of blood flow restriction training (BFRT) in the management of chronic non-specific neck pain: A scoping review.

Abstract.AbstractText: Muscle weakness and disuse from chronic neck pain can often lead to poor pain management and functional incapacity. Low-load resistance training is widely recommended, but it is less significant in improving muscle strength. Blood flow restriction training (BFRT) is reported to be an ambitious alternative to the call for an adjunct method to optimize the effects of low-load training.
The objective of this scoping review is to explore the research gaps, concerns of BFRT in the management of chronic non-specific neck pain conditions.

PRISMA-ScR was used to report this scoping review, which began on October 30, 2024. We searched EMBASE, CINAHL, MEDLINE (PubMed), Web of Science, Scopus, and PEDro database. Studies examining the effects of BFR or BFRT versus conventional or standard care exercise programs on the biochemical outcomes, muscle strength, pain intensity, and disability were included.

Eighty-nine articles were identified, and twenty-three (19 RCT, 3 quasi-experimental, and 1 case series) were included. Most studies evaluated upper extremity muscle strength, muscle cross-sectional area, muscle mass, and hormonal functions. However, the prescribed training procedures and BFRT cuff parameters were inconsistent across the studies.

While the evidence is still inconclusive and heterogeneous, BFRT (Blood Flow Restriction Training) appears to improve muscle mass, muscle strength, and hormonal function. To definitively prove the safe and effective clinical implication of BFRT for treating chronic neck pain, more trials are warranted.
